jsonLdWeb.name: Markup Calculator
jsonLdWeb.description: Calculate selling price from cost and markup percentage. Three modes: cost+markup, cost+price, and price+markup.

acceptedAnswer.text: Markup is the percentage added to the cost of a product to determine the selling price. If a product costs $50 and you apply a 40% markup, the selling price is $70. Markup is calculated as (Price - Cost) / Cost × 100.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between markup and margin?

acceptedAnswer.text: Markup is the percentage of the cost that is added as profit: (Price - Cost) / Cost × 100. Margin is the percentage of the selling price that is profit: (Price - Cost) / Price × 100. A 50% markup equals a 33.3% margin. Markup is always higher than margin for the same product.

mainEntity.name: How do you calculate selling price from cost and markup?

acceptedAnswer.text: Selling Price = Cost × (1 + Markup% / 100). For example, if cost is $80 and markup is 25%, selling price = $80 × 1.25 = $100.

mainEntity.name: What is a good markup percentage?

acceptedAnswer.text: It varies by industry. Grocery stores: 5-15%. Retail clothing: 50-100%. Restaurants: 200-300% on food. Jewelry: 100-300%. Electronics: 10-30%. The right markup depends on your overhead costs, competition, and target profit margin.

mainEntity.name: How do I convert markup to margin?

acceptedAnswer.text: Margin = Markup / (1 + Markup). For example, a 50% markup (0.50) gives a margin of 0.50 / 1.50 = 33.3%. Conversely, Markup = Margin / (1 - Margin). A 33.3% margin gives 0.333 / 0.667 = 50% markup.

mainEntity.name: Why is markup always higher than margin?

acceptedAnswer.text: Because markup is based on the smaller number (cost) while margin is based on the larger number (selling price). Since selling price is always higher than cost for profitable products, the same dollar profit divided by cost (markup) will always yield a higher percentage than when divided by price (margin).

howItWorks:
How to Calculate Markup

Markup is the amount added to the cost price of a product to determine the selling price. It is expressed as a percentage of cost.

Selling Price = Cost × (1 + Markup% / 100)

Important distinction — Markup vs. Margin:

  • Markup = (Price − Cost) / Cost × 100
  • Margin = (Price − Cost) / Price × 100
  • A 50% markup = 33.3% margin
  • A 100% markup = 50% margin

Example: Cost $50 with 40% markup → Price = $50 × 1.40 = $70.00 (margin = 28.6%)
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

What is markup?

Markup is the percentage added to the cost of a product to arrive at the selling price. If a product costs $50 and you apply a 40% markup, the selling price is $70. The formula is: Markup % = (Selling Price − Cost) / Cost × 100.

What is the difference between markup and margin?

Markup is the profit as a percentage of cost. Margin is the profit as a percentage of selling price. A 50% markup always equals a 33.3% margin. Markup is always a higher number than margin for the same product because cost (the denominator for markup) is smaller than price (the denominator for margin).

How do you calculate selling price from cost and markup?

Multiply the cost by (1 + markup percentage / 100). For example, if cost is $80 and markup is 25%, selling price = $80 × 1.25 = $100. The profit is $20 per unit.

What is a good markup percentage?

It varies widely by industry. Grocery stores typically use 5–15%. Retail clothing uses 50–100%. Restaurants mark up food 200–300%. Jewelry can be 100–300%. Electronics are usually 10–30%. The right markup depends on your overhead costs, competition, and desired profit margin.

How do I convert markup to margin?

Use the formula: Margin = Markup / (1 + Markup). Express both as decimals. For example, a 50% markup (0.50) gives a margin of 0.50 / 1.50 = 0.333 = 33.3%. To go the other way: Markup = Margin / (1 − Margin).

Why is markup always higher than margin?

Because markup uses cost as its base (the smaller number), while margin uses selling price (the larger number). The same dollar amount of profit divided by the smaller cost will always produce a larger percentage than when divided by the larger selling price.
